title: Pamela Weber -Director's Interview - Megan | Managing Editor
meeting_title: Pamela Weber -Director's Interview - Megan | Managing Editor

transcript.text: I'm like, gosh, that's a COVID thing.
This meeting is being recorded.
I want to get that t-shirt.
Right.
For sure.
How was your day so far?
Good, except this hour change messes with me every year.
I mean, it's like the spring one is worse, but even the fall one, it's just...
And my micro clock is cracked and oven's not, so it's like they're right next to each and it's like, yeah, but...
For sure.
Where are you dates?
I'm in Minnesota.
Oh, okay.
Nice.
I will actually be there later this month, visiting, yeah, my in-laws, so, yeah.
I'm in the northern part of the state.
I'm guessing further.
I'm from Minneapolis.
It's more interesting for it.
Yeah.
You're not supposed agree.
Well, I haven't been, so I'm just like, that would also be my impression, but I...
I'm from Fargo, which is right across from where I'm from, so yeah, but it's...
I like the whole state.
The state's awesome.
Oh, something's blocking.
is that?
Oh, they were recording.
There you go.
Well, actually, you're lucky.
It's a pretty mild winter so far.
We're going to...
Like maybe some frost this weekend.
mean, it's like, there's no snow on the ground.
It's like sunny and nice out.
It's like 57, which is unheard of this time of year.
We had a really mild winter like this a years ago.
And so, yeah, it's crazy.
It's like going to be like 35, maybe frost, maybe a little bit of light snow, but nothing major.
Yeah, nice, nice.
Where are you that it's going to be a shock, like with climate?
Where are you now?
I'm in San Francisco.
And we don't really have real seasons.
I went to college out there.
And I watch people wearing like earmuffs and stuff on 50 degrees.
I'm like, that's cold, people.
Minus 50.
Put a negative in front of it.
So, yeah.
I know.
I have my little heater.
I have my heater on for my feet right now because I'm like, oh, it's cold.
So maybe I won't do well, but we'll see.
It'll be fine.
It's another year, yeah, but so far it's mild.
So you'll be fine this year.
Okay, good, good.
Yeah, well, thank you so much for taking the time.
Yeah, really, yeah, really excited to get to chat.
Um,
Yeah, and just a bit about myself.
So I'm a director here at GrowthX.
And so I have about 11 clients that I work with and then have a team of three managing editors.
And then those managing editors have a couple of content managers helping to actually produce for those accounts.
Yeah, and so I guess, yeah, just to sort of kick things off.
Yeah, I'm well, yeah, I guess so.

Yeah, for for this conversation, I'm hoping just like hear a little bit about like how you've dealt with, like, managing like multiple accounts, like content quality control, and then just like collaboration with with teams internally.
So yeah, just to kick things off.
Would you be able to describe a time when you were managing multiple projects or accounts with competing deadlines and how you organized your your work in your processes?
Well, what I've found, and like now I'm in CSS, and our sister company is Edgy Digital Marketing Agency, which we worked with clients like Toyota and sales and all that.
So the deadlines are both sides.
have that.
One is like client-facing versus internal.
By the way, things need to go out when they need to go out.
I found the best way is to get ahead of it.
And like we use ClickUp.
And so when I, when I scope out a project, I let all the stakeholders know, I put in the card, all the information that they need, different, you know, the brief and different like assets.
And then I take each person and say, this is when I need this from you by this deadline.
I need for the next person, graphic designer, I need this image asset by this deadline.
So everybody in the card sees the whole workflow.
They have everything they need access to.
And then if you have any questions, let me know from the beginning.
And so it's all scoped out in the beginning and clear each actor what they need to deliver at what time.
And then I'll keep up with that.
And if there's something I haven't gotten, then I'll just like take them in there.
Reminder, we need this by the end of day at this time.
Are there any blockers or anything I can help you with?
So it's just about communication.
Being really clear with all the stakeholders.
then sometimes somebody will have a competing priority and it's something that came in.
And so then it's really just like communicating and just being clear and say, okay, which is the top priority?

And you have to really decide between not just within your own team, but between teams across departments, which is the one that's most important for the company this time, like to push the company forward or our goals.
So we'll like talk through that and say, okay, well, you need this by Thursday, end of day.
I really need it at that time, but I can push it to Friday by noon if that works.
So it's just about negotiating, being, you know, clear about, you know, what you need.
And then if they come up with something that just came last minute, we just rework that in a way that everybody can win.
And, you know, every team does it.
It's not about an individual to me.
I don't like when somebody is like, no, I need my deadline.
No, we all need our deadlines.
It's like a group effort, everything, even though we're individually working on it.
You know what I mean?
We need to like, I think as a team, so I think as a company, I think as a department.
So it's just about making everything, you know, being very clear from the outset and just doing little check-ins.
And so.
Sometimes I'll do a check-in before something's not delivered, you know, get a heads up on it.
Especially if I see a graphic designer, he's really busy and I'm feeling he may not get out of time.
Hey, are you going to be able to still deliver this by time?
And then we can, like, talk about it before we get to that point.
So, yeah, so just basically clear and, you know, just like organized on top of things.
I love ClickBub or, you know, I love all.
I love Monday, too.
It's so colorful.
Well, I have, even for personal, I'm such a dork.
Like, I have my own personal, like, organization system.
I love that.
I do Notion.
I want it Monday, and I do all this research, and they don't have a personal version.
I would so mind.
yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Internally, yeah.
Internally, use, yeah, we use Notion.
We use Linear mostly for, like, actual, like, ticket tracking.
But then, like, I personally like to use Asana just for, like, my own, my own processes.
But, yeah, it's just, yeah.
Nice.
Cool.
And then also, yeah, just quick follow-up on that.
Like, how do you go about determining what to prioritize?
How like, terms of, like, one client versus another client?
Oh, well, that's a tough one.
Well, clients are equally, they're all paying the bills, they're keeping the plates on.
So I guess, I guess you have a bigger client and, you know, bigger budget and, like, they are, or they're more pressing or it's, like, more important to their business goals.
Maybe prioritize that.
But I would still try and get everything as close as possible as when, you the client needs it.
What's the most important to the business?
Mm-hmm, yeah, for sure, for sure.
Cool.
And then, could you tell me about a time when maybe you, like, caught a major issue with, like, a piece of content before it was delivered to the customer?
And, like, what exactly did you catch?
And then how did you catch it?
I'm trying to think through.
I have such QA built in.
And, like, I really follow the writing process.
have such respect for it.
Like, I give space between when the final draft is done.
And then going back and, you know, doing a final review to make sure that I catch things.
So it's so rare that that comes up.
I'm like the finalized on all content.
So it's like maybe someone else missed something.
You know, when I reach, sometimes I'll be on a call and a screen will come up and I'll see like an error or something.
And I like DM like, can you just please fix it?
it's driving my eyes, my eyes twitching.
And it's not even something that went out.
It's something we're literally looking at.
And so I guess I'll catch things like that.
Yeah.
And I'll just, I was like, how did you see that?
Nobody else on the call saw it.
They're talking about something else.
But it's just like those like little things.
My eye just goes to that constantly.
Like even, you know, not work.
So I can't think, I'm sorry to, I can't think specifically about one major thing.
But it's just always, I'm always like on, you know, as I can't turn off.
I'm always checking to make sure everything's perfect and all content is correct.
And I can't think of one particular time.
But it's like everything that goes out, I make sure everything is correct.
So.
Yeah.
And it sounds like you said that you sort of have like your own process where like there's always going to be like Like a check.
Like
A good amount of time before it's actually supposed to be delivered to the client so that there is time to actually maintain that quality standard.
That's definitely.
Nice.
And then, let's see.
So would you be able to describe a time when a boss or other leader at the company gave you critical feedback, either on a workflow or a priority or a deliverable and how you handled that?
Sure.
And I love feedback.
A lot of people feel like constructive criticism is like they take it personally.
I don't.
It's going back to the writing thing because like writers, I've had a friend who would like often see me in the humanities building at San Francisco State.
And he's like, why are you always sitting in a circle?
He's an econ person and all the chairs are forward.
It's just like that, you know, that for, you know, teacher dumps the information in a student kind of view of their way of how they thought.
We saw it as like a circular thing.
Like we learn from each other and brainstorming.
It's a creative process.
So I see feedback as a great feedback loop.
I like to get better as a writer at my work.
So I welcome it.
I think it's great.
I think the only thing is one time we shifted on a content campaign, the keyword research really came in.
We use AI for part of it.
And then right when we were going to start drafting, our CTO looked at it and he's like an SEO expert.
And he said, this isn't the right direction for this.
And we were almost going to start, you know, we can always pull up, you know, like once you join the drafts and outline, it's like you're getting more and more time in.
And he said, this is not really matching user intent.
And it was something kind of close, like on the edge.
Like when I looked at it again, it's like, oh yeah, I can see that.
But it was so close that you could have like really put out either way.
But he's such an expert in that, that he saw where it was going.
And it may have shifted the traffic a bit from what our goal was for that keyword research strategy.
So something like that.
And then we just, we went with the different keywords and it worked fine.
So it was just a matter of, you know, just repositioning and pivoting.
You have to do a lot in startup.
That's what I learned.
Everything's kind of, we've worked so much, we're back to where we started.
For sure.
Yeah, no, absolutely.
Yeah, lots of pivoting, keeping you on your toes, for sure.
Yeah, nice.
Okay, yeah, that all sounds good.
And so, yeah, I guess, do you have any questions for me at all, or anything else that you feel like I should know about you and your background?
I know you've already talked to a bunch of other people on the team.
Before we could do anything else, is this wallpaper?
What's this beautiful thing behind you?
It's just like, is it like panels?
I love it.
I just can't figure it out.
They are panels.
It's awesome.
Yeah, I got them from an Etsy store.
It's just like these little partition walls.
Love them.
I just gave a poem as a gift to someone.
I'm a writer, but I'm not, well, duh, mean, you know that.
But I'm not like a visual person.
I mean, I appreciate it, but I can't like, so I had to work with a graphic designer, like with the color and the font.
And all that to make it how I wanted.
And from what she

At the end, to what I originally sent, I don't know how, she's like a miracle worker, she was like, it was exactly what I wanted, so anyway, so yeah, I love Etsy, it's so pretty, okay, so yeah, so back to your questions, you know what I really, I mean, yeah, he was really thorough, so I really have a great understanding of it, but also, like, this is more of a perfect fit than I originally understood from the original job posting, because it's like a marriage of client and AI content marketing, which is like literally my last world the last six years, because these are a Jesus sister company to the SaaS right now, it's the same three co-founders, bootstrapped, which is why, we have do everything, know, three hats, so it's like, I have that client side, I have, you the writing, obviously, side, I used to teach college English, I mean, I'm in the content market for years, and then I have the AI side, which is like the last five years, first I've learned how to use AI efficiently with InkFast, and then we rolled out an agent, SmithOS, so I've had, I came into both of those for great success, so I like learn just by doing, and it's like so much fun, Megan, I like, I have no idea humanity brain was going to get this techie, but I did, I don't even recognize myself some days, but yeah,
Anyway, so yeah, so I hear you.
Yeah, for sure.
Yeah, my background is in journalism.
So yeah, and like, I didn't think that I would be.
That's what I love about this company, too.
Because I like stocked.
I mean, I looked up all your LinkedIn.
And everybody has like, really great content background and writing, and like appreciation for real hardcore, you know, journalism, like, which is really what I wanted to go into.
But at state, you had to be like, accepted or something like a whole different field.
So I just went to English, but you wanted to be in the journalism class.
And they said, you have to like, you have be asked for something.
I was like, what?
I think you just made your signups.
Anyway, so I was already too late and already in, and I just did English.
But yeah, I just appreciate journalism.
It's like such a craft, you know, like writing.
like, it's just, it's just so beautiful.

And like, so I love that everybody at this company has like that, that appreciation of real writing, which makes your job easier when you're doing content, because everybody don't mind if you gets it.
You know, it's a different kind of art.
It's not just marketing, like the CMO and like the marketing bros are like, oh, well, this is just content.
Just put it together.
No, it's not just that.
You know, think about audience and persona and message.
And it's not just a few words, know, especially the AI, oh, just spit out AI.
It's like, it's more than that.
So, yeah, so I think it's part of why I'm so excited about this.
And as far as questions, yeah, I guess the only big thing I had, like, what is, like, the biggest content challenge, like, you're looking to solve with this role?
Or, like, what's not even solved, but what's, like, the biggest thing if I would start, like, in the first 30 days or 60, that would be, like, the best thing that I could do.
Beyond that, I understand the scope of what you're doing and how the workflow, but just, like, what is, like, the top thing that you really need in this role?
Yeah, I mean, I would say, yeah, it's, like, that, it's that editorial judgment, but also the ability to manage all of the content workflows.
And, like, I think, I think one, because I think ultimately, like, that's what we need, because it's just like you said, like, the AI itself, it's not perfect.
And it, it still needs, it needs a lot of help, even though we all want to think that, like, AI is, like, replacing humans.
It's not replacing us.
If
I'm going to hold my computer out the window.
Yeah, it's like humans are still very much needed and we need that insight, we need that overview.
So I think that is really ultimately what we're looking for.
But also just shaping those assignments, having that editorial judgment to determine what is the right piece of content based on the strategy that has been set forth.
But then also being able to look at some of that performance and then make adjustments as needed and be willing to pivot if something's not working to move towards something that is working.
Awesome.
That sounds exactly like what I've been doing and what I love doing.
Yeah.
Awesome.
Great.
Cool.
Well, yeah.
Any other questions for me?
We actually went overtime last time.
So I like.
We got a really good background on it.
you do chat with Matthew last time?
Yeah, nice.
Yeah, cool.
Awesome.
He said can have you fact-checked that he basically is full of it, is what he says.
Yeah, well, so Matthew and I go way back.
We actually, he was the editor-in-chief at TechCrunch.
I was a reporter at TechCrunch, like, years ago.
And so, yeah, so, like, he's just always been my boss wherever I go.
In every lifetime.
That's funny.
Yeah, but he's, like, he's a wealth of, he's a wealth of knowledge, a wealth of information.
And so, yeah, yeah, I'm glad you were able to go deep with him.
Yeah, this is, like, it's an awesome role.
It's, like, literally exactly what I love doing and an awesome company.
And everybody I met so far is great.
So, yeah, I'm really excited about this.
Cool.
Yeah, and so, yeah, in terms of next steps, we're going to ask you to do a, like, a take-home assignment.
But I still need to, like, touch base with the rest of the team.
And then Murilia will follow up with you, hopefully.
Yeah, I'm actually not sure about Timeline, but we'll definitely keep you in the loop.
Okay, sounds good.
Awesome.
All right, well, thanks so much, Pamela.
Really appreciate you taking the time.
And yeah, it was just, yeah, really great to chat.
Me too.
Thanks.
was nice meeting you and have fun in Minnesota.
Bring us some gloves and a hat because it's not technically cold for us, but, you know.
It'll be cold for me.
But have fun.
Everybody, it's Minnesota nice, it's true.
And we'll hold doors.
Because I remember California, I told the door, people would walk through a hundred people like, I don't work here.
So yeah, it's a big thing.
One time I was at Starbucks two days ago, two people, one was holding a door for this one and she was holding a door for him.
And I said, that's the most Minnesota thing I've ever seen.
They were each holding a door for the other person.
So yeah, so that's just, that's just a, that's a big tip.
We just, we're a door culture.
But other than that, have fun.
You'll love it.
Have a great trip.
Thanks, Pamela.
Thanks.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interviewing Pamela Weber for the Managing Editor role.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=113.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Proactive Management:** Pamela uses ClickUp to prevent deadline conflicts by scoping projects upfront, assigning clear deadlines to all stakeholders, and conducting proactive check-ins.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=166.0)
  - [**Systemic Quality Control:** A built-in QA process, including a deliberate review gap after drafting, ensures high-quality content. Pamela's attention to detail is so high that she often spots minor errors on shared screens during calls.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=392.0)
  - [**Agile & Collaborative:** Views feedback as a "great feedback loop" for improvement. Successfully pivoted a campaign's keyword strategy based on CTO feedback to better match user intent, preventing wasted effort.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=494.0)
  - [**Ideal Fit:** The role's blend of client management, content strategy, and AI marketing directly matches Pamela's experience from her current dual-role position at CSS/Edgy Digital.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)

## Topics

### The Challenge: Managing Competing Deadlines

  - [**Context:** Manages deadlines for both internal SaaS projects (CSS) and external agency clients (Edgy Digital), including Toyota and Salesforce.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=166.0)
  - [**Solution:** Proactive project scoping in ClickUp.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=195.0)
      - [Creates a project card with a full brief and all necessary assets.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=195.0)
      - [Assigns specific deadlines to each stakeholder (e.g., writer, designer).](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=195.0)
      - [This provides full workflow visibility, preventing surprises and enabling early intervention.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=195.0)
  - [**Conflict Resolution:**](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=241.0)
      - [Negotiates priorities by asking which project best serves company goals.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=241.0)
      - [Seeks win-win solutions (e.g., pushing a deadline from Thursday EOD to Friday noon).](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=241.0)
      - [Emphasizes collaboration over rigid adherence to individual deadlines.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=274.0)

### The Solution: Systemic Quality Control

  - [**Process:** A built-in QA process prevents major errors.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=392.0)
      - [Includes a deliberate review gap between final draft completion and final review.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=414.0)
      - [This creates mental distance, allowing for a fresh perspective to catch overlooked issues.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=414.0)
  - [**Attention to Detail:** Pamela's high standard for quality is constant.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=456.0)
      - [Frequently spots minor errors on shared screens during calls and flags them for correction, even before content is published.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=432.0)

### The Mindset: Agile & Collaborative

  - [**Feedback:** Views constructive criticism as a "great feedback loop" for professional growth.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=494.0)
  - [**Example:** A content campaign's keyword strategy, partly generated by AI, was flagged by the CTO as not matching user intent.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=548.0)
      - [**Action:** Pamela pivoted the strategy to new keywords, preventing wasted effort on an ineffective approach.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=548.0)

### The Fit: Pamela's Background & Role Alignment

  - [**Experience:** Current role at CSS/Edgy Digital combines client management, content strategy, and AI marketing.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
      - [**AI:** Learned efficient AI use with InkFast and helped roll out the SmithOS agent.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
  - [**Role Alignment:** The position's requirements directly match Pamela's experience and interests.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
      - [**Editorial Judgment:** Shaping assignments based on strategy and pivoting as needed.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=858.0)
      - [**Workflow Management:** Overseeing the full content production process.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=809.0)
      - [**Human Oversight:** Providing the critical human insight needed to refine AI-generated content.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=809.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Megan:** Discuss interview with the team.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=950.0)
  - [**Murilia:** Follow up with Pamela regarding a take-home assignment.](https://fathom.video/share/UtbK_QmjzEAWpCBNudwYeKW-cGersTM7?tab=summary&timestamp=950.0)

action_items.description: Align w/ team on Pamela take-home; then Murilia send assignment to Pamela
